feature_name: Dual operation modes: Root Complex and End Point
description: The PCIe module is dual-mode and can operate either as a Root Complex (RC) or as an End Point (EP). Mode is selected at boot using PCIESSMODE[1:0] straps and can be overridden in software via device status registers. RC mode provides Type 1 configuration access and downstream enumeration, while EP mode exposes Type 0 configuration and BAR-mapped application space. The application registers are accessible in both modes through Address Space 0 and BAR0. Software must select and lock the intended mode before enabling link training to avoid undefined behavior.
dependencies: PCIESSMODE[1:0] straps; DEVSTAT; CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]
Remarks: Set mode before LTSSM enable and leave unchanged while the link is active.

feature_name: Link capabilities: x1/x2 lanes and Gen1/Gen2 speeds
description: A single bidirectional link supports up to two lanes (×2) with raw speeds of 2.5 or 5.0 GT/s per lane. Automatic width conversion in the PHY allows Gen2 operation while keeping the PLL output at 2.5 GHz. The controller supports automatic lane reversal and RX polarity inversion to aid routing flexibility. Negotiated speed and width are visible in LINK_STAT_CTRL/LINK_CAP. After initial Gen1 link-up, software can request a directed speed change to Gen2 using PL_GEN2 and LINK_CTRL2 fields.
dependencies: SerDes PLL locked; COMMON_CLK per Section 2.2; PL_GEN2/LINK_CTRL2 set
Remarks: Ensure equalization and common-clock settings match platform constraints when enabling Gen2.

feature_name: Supported and unsupported PCIe transactions
description: The module implements most PCIe requests including memory read/write, configuration read/write (Type 0/1), and messages with and without data. Inbound I/O read/write transactions are not supported and must be avoided in RC and EP modes. Locked Memory Read requests and their Locked completions are not supported. Completion formatting for non-posted requests follows the baseline specification. Software should map only supported types through inbound/outbound translators to prevent UR or CA completions.
dependencies: Correct Type field in TLPs; address translators configured
Remarks: Do not target inbound I/O to this device and avoid Locked Read sequences.

feature_name: Address Space 0: application, local/remote config, and RC I/O window
description: Address Space 0 is a 16 KB region divided into four 4 KB subregions: application registers, local configuration space (Type 0/1), remote configuration proxy, and RC-only I/O window. All accesses to these regions must be 32-bit, non-burst, and non-cacheable. The remote configuration and I/O windows convert internal-bus reads/writes into PCIe configuration and I/O TLPs when in RC mode. Software must not access the remote windows while the link is down to avoid transaction timeouts. The I/O base for outbound I/O TLPs is provided by IOBASE.
dependencies: Operational link; IOBASE and CFG_SETUP programmed
Remarks: Implement link-up checks before touching remote windows to prevent system stalls.

feature_name: Outbound address translation (32 regions, 1–8 MB each)
description: Outbound translation partitions the device-internal space into 32 equal regions of 1, 2, 4, or 8 MB using OB_SIZE. For each indexed region, OB_OFFSET_INDEXn and OB_OFFSETn_HI supply the PCIe base address (32/64-bit) used for mapping. Translation applies only to memory read/write TLPs and not to configuration or I/O cycles. Transactions must be sized and aligned to avoid crossing region boundaries, as the mapping is determined only at command issue. Software should select a region size that matches buffer granularity to minimize splits.
dependencies: CMD_STATUS[OB_XLT_EN]=1; OB_* tables configured; EDMA/CPU access
Remarks: Do not exceed the remaining bytes within a region; choose larger regions for large buffers.

feature_name: Inbound address translation (4 regions) and BAR0 exception
description: Inbound translation remaps accepted PCIe addresses to internal bus addresses using up to four regions. Each region is associated with a BAR via IB_BARn and defines a PCIe start and internal offset via IB_STARTn_LO/HI and IB_OFFSETn. BAR0 is always mapped to the application registers in Address Space 0 and cannot be remapped. For 64-bit addressing, BAR pairs [2–3] and [4–5] map to Address Space 1 and are eligible for inbound data windows. Software must ensure inbound transactions do not cross 4 KB boundaries per the PCIe specification.
dependencies: CMD_STATUS[IB_XLT_EN]=1; BARs and IB_* programmed
Remarks: Use explicit IB_STARTn_* starts; reserve BAR0 strictly for control and status access.

feature_name: BAR Mask overlay and DBI_CS2-controlled configuration
description: BAR Mask registers overlay the BARs to predefine size/type/enable before RC enumeration. Writes to the masks are allowed only when CMD_STATUS[DBI_CS2]=1 and are write-only, requiring indirect verification via pattern writes to BARs. BAR0 mask is limited to bits [31:20] because BAR0 is fixed to application registers. The feature works in both RC and EP modes from the internal bus side only. Software must clear DBI_CS2 before enumeration to expose the intended BAR attributes to the host.
dependencies: CMD_STATUS[DBI_CS2] gating; internal bus writes; pattern readback
Remarks: Never modify BAR masks from the serial side; confirm mask effects by readback of BARs.

feature_name: Endianness conversion for inbound and outbound traffic
description: Although PCIe is little-endian on the wire, the subsystem supports big-endian CPU systems using programmable swizzle. The ENDIAN register selects the swap granularity (1/2/4/8 bytes) to correctly align FBE/LBE with data on the 64-bit internal bus. For MMR and non-prefetchable memory, swaps occur on 32-bit boundaries as required. The documentation provides explicit big-endian tables for inbound and outbound directions. Drivers must program ENDIAN consistently with CPU endianness and verify mixed-size transfers.
dependencies: System boot endianness; ENDIAN[1:0] programmed
Remarks: Validate with 1/2/4/8-byte operations; ensure contiguous byte enables align with chosen mode.

feature_name: DMA support via internal master/slave ports (no integrated DMA)
description: The PCIESS does not include a DMA engine but exposes a slave port for outbound EDMA traffic and a master port for inbound completions to memory. In RC mode, a system DMA can access any enumerated EP memory region over PCIe via the outbound translator. In EP mode, inbound PCIe writes reach the master port without additional DMA, while outbound reads/writes can be produced through the slave port. Sustained throughput targets approximately 85% of 2.0/4.0 Gbps per lane, accounting for 8b/10b and protocol overhead. TI provides EDMA examples for memory write and memory read sequences.
dependencies: EDMA configured; OB/IB translators set; STATUS_COMMAND[BUS_MS] if needed
Remarks: Align bursts to 128 B and avoid misaligned >120 B writes to meet bus constraints.

feature_name: Transaction requirements: alignment, size, bursts, and byte strobes
description: The internal bus limits a single transfer to 128 bytes and reduces to 120 bytes if not 8-byte aligned. Only incremental burst types are supported; fixed or wrap bursts can lead to undefined behavior or lockups. Write byte enables must be a single contiguous span of ones; holes are disallowed. Read responses are not interleaved when requests are sized at or below the configured maximum (128 B). Software and DMA engines must structure buffers and strides to honor these rules for reliability and performance.
dependencies: DMA burst configuration; DEV_STAT_CTRL[MAX_REQ_SZ,MAX_PAYLD] (Sec. 3.7.4)
Remarks: Design buffer alignments and transfer sizes around 128 B boundaries and contiguous strobes.

feature_name: Interrupt architecture: legacy INTx and MSI with 14 module events
description: The design supports legacy INTx generation in EP mode and reception in RC mode, and up to 32 MSI vectors for both EP and RC roles. MSI generation is achieved by issuing a memory write to the configured MSI address/data, or by writing the peer's BAR0 MSI_IRQ in supported scenarios. Internally, up to 14 interrupt sources aggregate MSI banks, legacy lines, error, and power/reset events to the SoC interrupt controller. Each MSI bank has enable/status/clear registers, and all events require an IRQ_EOI write with the event number to complete servicing. Drivers must avoid mixing INTx and MSI simultaneously for a function, as per capability rules.
evidence: Section 2.14; Table 2-10; MSI_CAP/MSI_LOW32/MSI_DATA (Sec. 3.6); MSI_IRQ, MSIn_* (Sec. 3.1.16, 3.1.24–3.1.55); IRQ_EOI (Sec. 3.1.15)
dependencies: MSI_CAP[MSI_EN]=1; MSIn_IRQ_ENABLE_SET; INTx disabled when MSI enabled
Remarks: Clear MSIn_IRQ_STATUS (write-1) and then write IRQ_EOI to avoid retrigger or missed vectors.

feature_name: Error handling and Advanced Error Reporting (AER)
description: Baseline PCI/PCIe error bits are exposed in STATUS_COMMAND and DEV_STAT_CTRL to enable reporting of correctable, non-fatal, fatal, and unsupported request errors. The AER extended capability provides detailed uncorrectable/correctable status, masks, severity, and header logs (HDR_LOG0–3) to capture failing TLP headers. Root ports can control SERR on various error classes and PME interrupting via ROOT_CTRL_CAP and ROOT_ERR_CMD. End-to-end CRC (ECRC) generation/checking is controllable through PCIE_ACCR and should be enabled only when peers support it. Software should classify, log, and clear W1C bits promptly, and tune mask/severity to suit application resilience and diagnostics.
dependencies: Enable *_RP bits; configure *_MASK/SVRTY; optionally ECRC per peer support
Remarks: Prefer disabling ECRC when peers do not support it; use first-error pointer for accurate triage.

feature_name: Power management: ASPM L0s/L1 and device/link state control
description: The controller supports ASPM transitions to L0s and L1, with control in LINK_CAP/LINK_STAT_CTRL and timing modifiers in ACK_FREQ. Device power states D0–D3 are implemented via PMCAP/PM_CTL_STAT, with D1/D2 optional and D3hot/D3cold behaviors defined. Software-driven transitions to L1 accompany device moves into D1/D2/D3hot for energy savings. The relationship between device and link states is enumerated to keep links coherent during power changes. PME messaging and related interrupts allow the RC/EP to coordinate wake-ups and shutdowns cleanly.
dependencies: Platform latency budget; COMMON_CLK_CFG; ACTIVE_LINK_PM settings
Remarks: Select ASPM classes consistent with system timing and use PME to coordinate transitions.

feature_name: Loopback capabilities: PIPE and PHY (RC) for test and compliance
description: PIPE loopback allows master/slave operation for symbol-level validation with test equipment; it cannot loop back TLP transactions. PHY loopback routes TX to RX internally and can exercise TLP paths without a partner, but is only supported in RC procedure. PHY loopback requires enabling RX/TX loopback in SERDES_CFGx, disabling RX_LOS as directed, and forcing LTSSM to POLL_ACTIVE via PL_FORCE_LINK. Configuration TLPs are not valid in PHY loopback, and BAR/base-limit settings must avoid capturing the traffic. To prevent DLL sequence mismatches, enable loopback before any live traffic is emitted.
dependencies: RC mode for PHY loopback; SerDes configured; CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]
Remarks: Use PHY loopback for bring-up/validation; employ PIPE loopback for link-layer compliance only.

feature_name: Reset mechanisms: POR, system reset, and software hot reset
description: Power-On Reset (POR) cold-resets the entire device including the PCIESS, while system reset supports hard and soft variants with sticky bits retained on soft reset. Software can issue a downstream hot reset using RSTCMD[INIT_RST] after ensuring no pending transactions (check FLUSH_N). After any reset, software must wait at least 100 ms before attempting PCIe transactions to allow link partners to recover. Power-domain cycling of the PCIe module resets state and requires re-initialization and link training. Improper power-down without quiescing traffic can hang the system until a full POR.
dependencies: PSC sequence; link quiescence protocols
Remarks: Honor timing requirements and re-execute initialization sequences after resets or domain cycles.

feature_name: SerDes PLL and reference clock configuration (KeyStone I)
description: The PHY includes a PLL that multiplies a differential reference clock (typically 100 MHz) to a 2.5 GHz line rate for both Gen1 and Gen2 operation. PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL selects loop bandwidth, VCO range, and MPY, and enables the PLL; PCIE_SERDES_STS reports lock and health. After ENPLL is set, allow ~1 μs for regulator stabilization, then expect PLL lock within ~200 reference cycles and LOCK asserted after an additional 2048–3071 cycles. Proper loop bandwidth selection (LB) depends on refclk quality (LJCB versus external cleaner PLL). PCIESS registers are only reliable once the PLL reports LOCK.
dependencies: Stable refclk; Kicker unlock for chip cfg; PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL.ENPLL=1
Remarks: Program MPY for refclk→2.5 GHz; tune LB per jitter profile; poll STS[LOCK] before LTSSM.

feature_name: Port/Link control and training parameters
description: Port Logic registers expose controls for link enable, loopback, hot-reset assertion, and message injection. LINK_CTRL2 allows selecting target speed, compliance patterns, de-emphasis, and TX margins; PL_GEN2 sets directed speed change, de-emphasis, TX swing, and lane enables. ACK_FREQ and SYM_NUM configure L0s/L1 entry timing, number of fast training sequences, and SKP insertion cadence. These controls are valuable for bring-up, interoperability tuning, and compliance testing. Some fields are sticky across hot reset and must be handled explicitly by software.
dependencies: Proper LTSSM sequencing; partner capability negotiation
Remarks: Use cautiously in production; document any non-default training or de-emphasis settings.

feature_name: Transaction filtering and policy controls (RC)
description: Filter-mask registers allow the RC to drop or relax checks for certain TLP classes, attributes, ECRC errors, and vendor-specific messages. Fields also let software treat out-of-BAR, poisoned, or function-mismatch requests as supported for controlled scenarios. These knobs are intended for lab and special deployments; default behavior enforces strict compliance and robust error handling. DLLP abort behavior, flush handling, and SKP spacing can also be tuned. Misuse may hide protocol violations and complicate debugging.
dependencies: RC role; detailed policy design
Remarks: Keep defaults in production; clearly gate any exceptions and log policy deviations.

feature_name: Initialization sequences (RC and EP)
description: Bring-up requires a strict order: supply clocks and power, select mode, configure SerDes, wait for PLL LOCK, keep LTSSM disabled during register setup, then enable LTSSM and wait for L0 (DEBUG0.LTSSM_STATE=0x11). RC then performs enumeration, BAR assignment, and downstream initialization; EP may use APP_RETRY_EN to return Retry during slow initialization. Configuration and memory windows must be defined before enabling bus mastering or DMA flows. Any speed change to Gen2 should occur only after stable L0 at Gen1. Deviations from this order risk link training failures and undefined side effects.
dependencies: PSC; SerDes; LTSSM; enumeration SW
Remarks: Automate checks for STS[LOCK] and LTSSM state; gate outbound traffic until windows are programmed.

feature_name: Hot-plug/slot capability fields (RC representation)
description: While no built-in hot-plug hardware exists, RC-mode registers expose slot capability and status/control for standards compliance. Fields cover presence detect, indicators, interlock, power controller, and command-complete/HP interrupts. These allow system software to reflect chassis wiring and manage user interactions. If the platform lacks the wiring, capabilities should be left cleared to prevent OS enabling hot-plug. Any use must be consistent with the physical implementation and power sequencing.
dependencies: Platform slot wiring and power control
Remarks: Do not advertise capabilities that are not physically implemented in the system.

feature_name: Physical pins and lanes (no pin multiplexing)
description: The PCIe interface exposes differential TX/RX pairs per lane (up to two lanes) and a differential reference clock input. Functional PCIe pins are not multiplexed with other peripherals; only mode/power bootstrap bits are latched via GPIO/Timer at POR. Proper SerDes supplies, grounds, and on-board terminations are required for signal integrity. Board layouts must follow TI SerDes guidance for impedance, coupling, and skew. Platform bring-up should verify refclk quality and eye margins on each lane.
dependencies: Board-level refclk source; SerDes rails and terminations
Remarks: Follow SI guidelines and validate with compliance and eye-diagram testing.

feature_name: Clock control and common-clock architecture
description: The PHY derives all internal bit and functional clocks from the differential reference clock using its PLL. Many systems employ a common REFCLK (often with SSC), which must be reflected by setting COMMON_CLK_CFG when shared with the link partner. If a common clock is not used, the number of training sequences increases and software must indicate separate clocking. PCIESS registers are accessible only after PLL LOCK; premature access can produce unreliable behavior. Spread-spectrum and jitter budgets must be honored to ensure robust link training and maintenance.
dependencies: Stable refclk and SSC profile; COMMON_CLK policy
Remarks: Set COMMON_CLK_CFG correctly and allow extra training when separate clocks are used.

feature_name: Feature limitations (unsupported capabilities)
description: The module does not support splitting a ×2 link into two ×1 links, multiple virtual channels, or multiple traffic classes. It lacks function-level reset, built-in hot-plug hardware, and in-band wake beacon. Inbound I/O access and non-incremental burst types are unsupported, and addresses must remain uncacheable in certain spaces. There is no auxiliary power retention for D3cold, and the L2 link state is not supported. System software must architect around these gaps using external switches, policy, or alternative flows where needed.
dependencies: System and board design choices
Remarks: Plan topology with external switches for fan-out; avoid wrap/fixed bursts and inbound I/O.
